research_interests: Refugee health; Chronic disease management; Displacement and health
research_interests_enriched: Tala Al‑Rousan’s research centers on the health of refugees and other displaced populations, with a particular emphasis on chronic disease management such as hypertension, cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, kidney disease, and dementia. She combines randomized controlled trials, mixed‑methods feasibility studies, and large‑scale epidemiologic analyses to develop and test culturally tailored self‑management and digital health interventions, while also investigating social determinants and aging among minority and underserved groups.

methodologies: randomized controlled trial (RCT); mixed methods study; qualitative interviews; cohort analysis; cross‑sectional epidemiologic study; community‑based participatory research; digital health intervention feasibility testing; population health surveys
disease_areas: hypertension; cardiovascular disease; metabolic syndrome; chronic kidney disease; end‑stage kidney disease; dementia; Alzheimer's disease; HIV‑associated neurocognitive impairment; secondary primary lung cancer; obesity
populations: refugees (e.g., Syrian, diverse origins); immigrants; displaced persons; older adults; minority aging populations; African immigrants; Hispanic/Latino communities; homeless patients with kidney disease; general US adult population

Her work also spans population‑level prevention programs such as school‑based tobacco control and capacity‑building seminars for epidemiologists.

Her work also explores vascular imaging biomarkers such as breast arterial calcification and addresses complex conditions including cardiogenic shock, mechanical circulatory support, and chronic thromboembolic disease.

research_interests_enriched: Victor De Gruttola develops and applies advanced statistical and machine learning methods to understand and control infectious diseases, with a focus on HIV and COVID‑19. He also investigates immunologic and neurocognitive outcomes in people living with HIV and evaluates behavioral interventions such as mindfulness‑based stress reduction.

research_interests: Alzheimer's disease (AD) etiology and risk factors; Genetic epidemiology of AD and related disorders; AD clinical trial design and methods
research_interests_enriched: Steven Edland’s research centers on the etiology, genetic epidemiology, and modifiable risk factors of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and related dementias, with a strong emphasis on optimizing clinical trial design and outcome measurement. He develops and validates novel cognitive composites, integrates genetic risk scores and plasma biomarkers (e.g., p‑tau) for trial enrichment, and investigates practice effects and statistical methods such as mixed‑effects and IRT models. His work also includes data‑management infrastructure, statistical consulting, and mentorship programs that promote diversity in aging research.

title: Assistant Professor
research_interests: Design, use, and impact of technologies on health; Digital mental health; Qualitative and mixed methods
research_interests_enriched: Dr. Elizabeth Eikey investigates the design, implementation, and real‑world impact of digital health technologies, with a primary focus on mental health tools and their integration into public behavioral health systems. Her work combines qualitative, mixed‑methods, and quantitative approaches to identify barriers and facilitators to adoption, emphasizing equity across diverse populations such as Latino adults with chronic spine pain and adolescents. She also examines how technology influences health behaviors like physical activity and mindfulness practice.

department: Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health
title: Associate Professor
research_interests: Community-engaged research; Social and structural determinants of infectious disease; Global health
research_interests_enriched: Rebecca Fielding‑Miller conducts community‑engaged, interdisciplinary research on the social and structural determinants of infectious diseases and health disparities, with a particular focus on HIV and COVID‑19. She integrates global health perspectives and school‑based health interventions, employing mixed‑methods and spatial analyses to understand how poverty, stigma, racism, and gender inequality shape testing, vaccination, and care linkage. Her work also extends to vulnerable populations such as high‑risk women in Tanzania, disabled caregivers, and adolescents in U.S. schools, linking epidemiologic surveillance (e.g., wastewater, genomic sequencing) to policy and practice.

methodologies: mixed‑methods research; qualitative interviews; surveys; cross‑sectional study; serial cross‑sectional study; spatial analysis; wastewater‑based epidemiology; genomic sequencing; case investigation; contact tracing; community‑based participatory research
disease_areas: HIV/AIDS; COVID‑19; heart transplant rejection; mental health (child and adolescent); sexual violence; caregiver burnout

His work bridges clinical trials, implementation science, and comparative effectiveness to inform policy and practice in integrative health.

His work also spans the social determinants of health in inflammatory bowel disease, natural‑history studies of congenital disorders of glycosylation, and immune mechanisms underlying viral arthritis, employing large‑scale cohort and cross‑sectional designs to develop and validate risk scores and novel diagnostic tools.

research_interests_enriched: Linda Hill’s research centers on injury prevention and transportation safety, with a strong emphasis on vulnerable and displaced populations. She investigates how health disparities, aging, and substance use affect driving behaviors and outcomes, and leads community‑based interventions to improve COVID‑19 testing and colorectal cancer screening in underserved health centers. Her work integrates public health, implementation science, and gerontology to develop evidence‑based strategies that enhance safety and health equity.

research_interests: Immune and inflammation biomarker research; Chronic stress and mental health outcomes; Cardiometabolic health and interventions
research_interests_enriched: Professor Suzi Hong investigates the interplay between immune and inflammatory biomarkers and mental health outcomes, focusing on chronic stress, depression, anxiety, and PTSD across the lifespan. Her work also examines cardiometabolic and vascular health, including hypertension and frailty, through interventions such as Tai Chi and by assessing environmental exposures like pesticide metabolites. She integrates neuro‑immune mechanisms in vulnerable populations such as adolescents, older adults, and young adults living with HIV.
expertise_keywords: immune biomarkers; inflammation; chronic stress; mental health; depression; anxiety; post‑traumatic stress disorder; neuro‑immune mechanisms; cardiometabolic health; hypertension; vascular baroreflex; autonomic regulation; Tai Chi intervention; pesticide exposure; urinary metabolite biomarkers; DNA methylation epigenetics; microbiome‑immune‑brain axis; aging; cognitive impairment; HIV‑associated mood disorders
methodologies: longitudinal cohort studies; cross‑sectional biomarker analysis; urinary and serum metabolite assays; inflammatory cytokine profiling; DNA methylation sequencing; physiological recordings (baroreflex, heart‑rate variability); randomized controlled trial (Tai Chi vs health education); retrospective cohort analysis; integrative computational modeling (MASSAG); animal (mouse) neurophysiology; statistical mediation and moderation modeling; systems biology approaches
disease_areas: depression; anxiety; post‑traumatic stress disorder; HIV‑related mood disorders; hypertension; cardiovascular disease; metabolic syndrome/obesity; Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias; cognitive impairment; environmental health effects of pesticides
populations: adolescents (including agricultural communities); young adults with HIV; older adults (community‑dwelling); Asian children and adolescents; Latino adults; pregnant women; mouse models

research_interests_enriched: Pooyan Kazemian’s research integrates artificial intelligence, machine learning, and advanced optimization techniques to improve medical decision‑making and health policy. He develops interpretable deep‑learning models for disease diagnosis (e.g., Alzheimer’s, COVID‑19) and designs data‑driven, distributionally robust optimization frameworks for resource allocation and surgical care planning. His work also spans causal inference and simulation approaches for chronic and infectious disease modeling, with a strong emphasis on translating computational methods into actionable health solutions.

research_interests_enriched: Donna Kritz‑Silverstein’s research integrates women’s health and chronic disease epidemiology with dental public health and education. She investigates cardiovascular, bone, and breast cancer outcomes in postmenopausal women, the long‑term effects of hysterectomy, and how nutrition (e.g., egg and potassium intake) influences cognitive aging and mortality. Parallel work examines dental students’ preparedness to serve underserved populations and evaluates educational interventions. Her studies employ large prospective cohorts, community‑based surveys, and objective health measures such as actigraphy.

research_interests_enriched: Sydney Leibel’s research integrates pediatric respiratory health, environmental exposures, and molecular mechanisms of disease. She investigates how climate‑driven air pollutants and aeroallergens influence asthma and other respiratory outcomes in children, while also exploring viral‑host interactions of SARS‑CoV‑2 and metabolic crosstalk in EGFR‑driven lung adenocarcinoma. Complementary work examines the microbiome and metabolome of preterm infants in relation to breastfeeding. Her interdisciplinary approach spans clinical epidemiology, virology, and cancer metabolism.

department: Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health

research_interests_enriched: Lin Liu’s research integrates advanced computational methods, such as graph machine learning and atlas‑guided analytics, with translational biomedical investigations. Her work spans environmental modeling of water discharge and nitrogen dynamics, immunological discovery of transcription factors for T‑cell programming, and clinical outcome studies on memory impairment in gynecologic cancer survivors, pulmonary arterial hypertension biomarkers, and migraine therapeutics. She also applies experimental plasma physics and structure‑activity relationship modeling to address mechanistic and drug‑discovery challenges in hemophilia joint health and novel anti‑cancer agents.

research_interests_enriched: Dr. Becky Marquez investigates obesity, nutrition, and physical activity within Latinx communities, emphasizing family‑based and culturally tailored interventions. Her work integrates behavioral therapy, technology‑enhanced programs, and qualitative approaches to improve weight management, diabetes prevention, and related health outcomes among Mexican‑American women, mother‑daughter dyads, and broader Latino families. research_interests_enriched: Dr. Sara McMenamin’s research focuses on the intersection of health‑insurance policy, tobacco and substance‑use prevention, and reproductive health among adolescents and young adults. She examines how state‑level benefit mandates and insurance design affect access to smoking‑cessation services, fertility‑preservation care for cancer survivors, and non‑pharmacologic pain treatments. Her work blends quantitative analyses of large cohort and claims datasets with implementation‑science approaches and randomized controlled trials to generate evidence for public‑policy decision making.

research_interests_enriched: Dr. Winta Mehtsun’s research centers on health services and epidemiologic investigations of racial and ethnic disparities across the cancer care continuum in the United States. Her work also explores perceived healthcare discrimination and its impact on health outcomes, aiming to inform policies that reduce inequities in cancer prevention, treatment, and survivorship.

research_interests_enriched: Dr. Lindsay Miller investigates the biological mechanisms linking chronic kidney disease, particularly kidney tubule dysfunction, to cognitive impairment and physical decline in older adults. Her work integrates proteomic profiling, kidney biomarker measurement (e.g., cystatin C, creatinine, tubular injury markers), and brain imaging within large longitudinal cohorts to identify sex‑specific pathways and health‑disparity factors. She also explores related aging outcomes such as frailty, hip fracture risk, and cardiovascular mortality.

research_interests_enriched: Steve Nguyen’s research centers on discovering and validating plasma proteomic and epigenetic biomarkers that connect physical activity, biological aging, and neurodegeneration. research_interests_enriched: Professor Michael Pratt’s research centers on the surveillance, measurement, and policy dimensions of physical activity at the global level. He leads the Global Observatory for Physical Activity (GoPA!) and its education counterpart (GoPE!), producing country‑specific data, bibliometric analyses, and policy evaluations to guide health promotion, active transportation, and school‑based physical education. His work also emphasizes capacity building for physical‑activity research in low‑ and middle‑income countries and translating evidence into public‑health strategies for diverse populations, including older adults.

research_interests_enriched: Vivian Reznik’s research centers on building a diverse and inclusive biomedical workforce and transforming academic medicine culture to promote health equity. Her work integrates education, policy, and implementation science to improve outcomes for underrepresented groups and vulnerable populations.

research_interests_enriched: Dr. Rany Salem’s research integrates genetic epidemiology and advanced statistical genetics to dissect the architecture of complex, multivariate and longitudinal phenotypes, with a primary focus on cardiometabolic diseases such as type 2 diabetes, diabetic kidney and liver complications, hypertension, and coronary artery disease. He develops and applies gene‑environment interaction, causal inference (including Mendelian randomization), and machine‑learning methods to large multi‑ethnic cohort and biobank datasets, extending his work to related conditions including autoimmune disorders, PTSD, melanoma, Alzheimer’s disease, and polycystic ovary syndrome.

methodologies: Genome‑wide association studies (GWAS); Exome‑wide sequencing; Multivariate and longitudinal phenotype modeling; Gene‑environment interaction analysis; Mendelian randomization / causal inference; Machine learning / deep learning for genomics; 3D genome folding analysis; Epigenome‑wide association studies (EWAS); Copy number variation detection; Meta‑analysis of cohort studies; Bioinformatics pipeline development

research_interests: Image analysis; High-dimensional data analysis; Time series
research_interests_enriched: Professor Armin Schwartzman develops and applies advanced statistical and computational methods—including high‑dimensional regression, spatial inference, multiple‑testing correction, and deep‑learning image analysis—to biomedical and environmental data. His work spans neuroimaging genetics for neuropsychiatric conditions such as autism, automated quality assessment and registration of liver MRI, and the spatial epidemiology of heat‑related hospitalizations, with extensions to atmospheric CO₂ verification. These approaches integrate time‑series and random‑field theory to uncover biomarkers and risk markers across diverse health domains.

disease_areas: autism spectrum disorder; neuropsychiatric conditions; liver disease (MRI assessment); heat‑related illness; cancer risk markers

department: Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health
title: Associate Professor
research_interests: Longevity; Geroscience; Women's health
research_interests_enriched: Dr. Aladdin H. Shadyab investigates the biological mechanisms of longevity and age‑related disease through a geroscience lens, with a particular emphasis on women’s health. His work integrates longitudinal cohort data, plasma and neuroimaging biomarkers, epigenetic aging clocks, and genetic analyses to elucidate risk factors for cognitive decline, Alzheimer’s disease, sarcopenia, and menopause‑related symptoms. He also examines how reproductive history, hormone therapy, and cancer survivorship influence late‑life health outcomes.

research_interests_enriched: Professor Stephen Waterman's research centers on the epidemiology of vector‑borne diseases and the assessment of vector‑control strategies, with a strong emphasis on vaccine‑preventable infections. His work integrates quantitative epidemiologic methods, field entomology, and cross‑border data collaboration to inform public‑health interventions.

disease_areas: vector‑borne diseases (e.g., malaria, dengue, Zika, Lyme disease, West Nile virus); vaccine‑preventable diseases (e.g., measles, influenza, HPV, COVID‑19); infectious diseases of border relevance (e.g., tuberculosis, HIV, emerging respiratory pathogens)
populations: border and binational communities; populations in endemic vector‑borne disease regions; travelers and migrant groups; general public in surveillance catchment areas

research_interests_enriched: Professor Shu‑Hong Zhu investigates population‑level strategies to reduce tobacco use and related health harms, emphasizing culturally tailored interventions such as Asian‑language quitlines and low‑income outreach. His work integrates policy analysis (e.g., tobacco tax impacts), school‑based prevention programs, and clinical support (e.g., NRT and quitline services for hospitalized smokers). He also examines cannabis use patterns among Asian American adolescents, linking substance use behaviors to broader health outcomes such as asthma and secondhand smoke exposure.

department: Herbert Wertheim School of Public Health
title: Health Sciences Assistant Clinical Professor (Voluntary)
research_interests_enriched: Sara Baird’s research bridges public health, gerontology, and mental health implementation science, focusing on how substance use, dietary supplement consumption, and mental health conditions affect driving safety and overall wellbeing in older adults and veteran populations. She employs mixed‑methods and hybrid effectiveness‑implementation trials to evaluate interventions such as dialectical behavior therapy for suicide prevention and employee‑based distracted‑driving programs. Her work also extends to translational cancer biology, characterizing prostate cancer stem‑cell–like lines to inform disease mechanisms. This interdisciplinary portfolio supports evidence‑based policy and clinical practice across aging, substance use, and mental health domains.

title: Professor

research_interests_enriched: Professor Peter Franks investigates how physical ocean processes—from turbulent mixing to sub‑mesoscale currents—shape marine microbial communities and ecosystem dynamics. His work integrates field observations, autonomous underwater robot drifters, and advanced image‑based classification to elucidate plankton behavior, harmful algal bloom formation, and the biogeochemical feedbacks linking ocean physics and biology. This interdisciplinary approach aims to improve prediction of bloom events and understand the broader implications for marine health and climate‑driven ecosystem change.

title: Professor
research_interests: Nano-bioelectronics; data analytics; nano/bio interface; miniaturized sensors for single cell detection
research_interests_enriched: Professor Zeinab Jahed’s research integrates nano‑bioelectronics and data analytics to develop miniaturized, single‑cell sensors and bio‑electronic platforms for real‑time intracellular monitoring. She focuses on the nano/bio interface, employing mechanochemical modeling, machine learning, and deep‑learning approaches to understand how nanoscale topography and mechanical forces regulate nuclear architecture, signaling pathways (e.g., YAP/TAZ), and cellular metabolism. Her work spans the design of nanosensors, nanoelectrode arrays, and multimodal imaging tools to interrogate cardiac electrophysiology, neuronal network activity, and periodontal health, with applications across human and animal systems.

methodologies: physics‑informed deep learning; machine learning classification; predictive mechanochemical modeling; nanosensor fabrication; nanoelectrode array recordings; multimodal imaging (chemical & biomedical); ultrasound imaging analysis; label‑free detection using morphological embeddings; dynamic spike sorting; in vitro single‑cell detection; computational modeling; data analytics pipelines

title: Professor
research_interests: Network and systems economics, game theory, machine learning
research_interests_enriched: Professor Tara Javidi’s research bridges theoretical and applied machine learning, focusing on network and systems economics, game theory, and online learning algorithms such as multi‑armed bandits and zero‑order optimization in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces. research_interests_enriched: Professor Garrison Jenkins focuses on the genetic architecture of psychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders using large‑scale genome‑wide association studies and multi‑ancestry meta‑analyses. He also integrates cognitive phenotyping to link genetic variation with neurobehavioral traits such as cognitive flexibility in mood disorders.

title: Professor
research_interests_enriched: Professor Xianfeng Jiang leads interdisciplinary research at the interface of neurobiology, biomedical data science, and molecular therapeutics. His work spans mechanistic studies of nucleocytoplasmic transport in ALS and motor‑imagery decoding for spinal cord injury using graph neural networks, while also developing governance frameworks and ethical design for health‑data AI/ML pipelines. He investigates genomic determinants of infectious agents such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis and explores novel molecular targets—including NTRK2 fusions, CD39, and ferroptosis pathways—to modulate tumor microenvironments and improve cancer therapy. Recent efforts also integrate materials science, exemplified by zeolite‑based catalysis for sustainable hydrogenation processes.

research_interests_enriched: Professor Yoav Kumagai’s research focuses on defect engineering and impurity doping in copper‑based semiconductor materials. research_interests_enriched: Jennifer Lewis, Distinguished Professor, focuses on the epidemiology, clinical outcomes, and therapeutic strategies for inflammatory bowel diseases, including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, with an emphasis on health disparities across ethnic groups. Her work also spans biomarker discovery for cancer and cardiovascular disease, development of multi‑cancer blood tests using extracellular vesicle proteins, and innovative clinical interventions in ophthalmology and neuroimaging. She integrates large‑scale cohort analyses, randomized controlled trials, and consensus guideline development to advance precision medicine.

research_interests_enriched: Professor Tzu Mao Li leverages expertise in computer graphics, image processing, and data science to develop computational and algorithmic solutions across biomedical and materials domains. He also applies computational modeling and inverse planning to cognitive science narratives and designs nanomaterial coatings and high‑temperature dielectric energy storage systems.

title: Professor
research_interests_enriched: Professor Guyue Song investigates the molecular mechanisms of environmental toxicants using vertebrate model systems, focusing on mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, ferroptosis, and calcium dysregulation in zebrafish and bovine mammary tissues. In parallel, he develops rapid immunological assays and isolates broadly neutralizing antibodies against SARS-like viruses, employing comparative genomics and high‑throughput sequencing to understand viral immunity. He also applies dense sampling comparative genomics to avian diversity for evolutionary and conservation insights.

title: Professor
research_interests_enriched: Professor Bryan Williams investigates the genetic and molecular underpinnings of neurodegenerative and neuropsychiatric disorders, with a focus on multi‑ancestry genome‑wide analyses of Alzheimer’s disease and transcriptional signatures of opioid addiction across human and rodent brains.  He also leads interdisciplinary work on women’s urological health, exploring bladder health, lower urinary tract symptoms, and overactive bladder through epidemiologic studies, qualitative research, and public‑health messaging.  His portfolio integrates digital health interventions for vulnerable populations such as adolescent mothers and applies advanced therapeutic techniques, including laser interstitial thermal therapy for radiation necrosis in brain metastases.

title: Professor
research_interests: Physical oceanography, internal waves, ocean mixing, turbulence
research_interests_enriched: Professor Matthew Alford investigates the physical processes that drive ocean mixing, focusing on internal waves, turbulence, and diapycnal fluxes across a range of marine environments from the Arctic to the South China Sea. His work integrates in‑situ microstructure measurements, distributed optical‑fiber sensing, and moored profiling to quantify turbulent heat and salt fluxes, tidal dissipation, and sediment transport, and he also contributes to the development of internal‑wave mixing parameterizations for climate models. Earlier in his career he explored neurobiological mechanisms of Alzheimer’s disease, studying amyloid‑beta dynamics and apolipoprotein E signaling in animal models.

research_interests: Deep-sea ecology, benthic ecology, oxygen minimum zones, hydrothermal vents, methane seeps
research_interests_enriched: Lisa Levin investigates the ecology and biogeochemistry of deep‑sea benthic habitats, with particular emphasis on methane seeps, hydrothermal vents, and oxygen‑minimum zones. Her work integrates field experiments using submersibles and ROVs with laboratory physiological and geochemical analyses to understand how extreme chemical environments shape chemosynthetic communities, larval development, and connectivity across continental margins. She also explores the impacts of ocean warming, deoxygenation, and acidification on marine larvae and the broader carbon cycle, linking modern observations to paleo‑ecological contexts such as the Cambrian radiation.
expertise_keywords: deep‑sea ecology; benthic ecology; methane seeps; hydrothermal vents; oxygen minimum zones; chemosynthetic communities; larval physiology; ocean acidification; larval connectivity; biogeochemical cycling; authigenic carbonate formation; extreme sulfide habitats; macroinvertebrate community dynamics; paleo‑ecology; Cambrian radiation; geochemical proxies; metal isotope geochemistry
methodologies: submersible and ROV deployments; in situ colonization experiments; field sampling of deep‑sea sediments and fauna; laboratory physiological assays (e.g., vision under low O₂); larval rearing and exposure experiments (pH, O₂); stable isotope and metal isotope geochemistry; geochemical proxy development; molecular ecology techniques; connectivity modeling and population genetics; numerical modeling of carbon and nutrient fluxes
populations: marine invertebrate larvae (bivalves, gastropods, echinoderms); deep‑sea macroinvertebrates (cusk‑eels, Dorvilleidae worms); chemosynthetic microbial communities; benthic megafauna at methane seeps and hydrothermal vents; Pacific continental margin ecosystems; Costa Rica methane seep fields; Southern California methane seep sites; Chilean deep‑sea seep habitats; Argentine deep‑sea basins

research_interests_enriched: Professor Peter Franks investigates the coupled physical‑biological dynamics of marine ecosystems, focusing on plankton and zooplankton population processes across scales from microscale patchiness to basin‑wide climate forcing. His work integrates autonomous underwater robotics, high‑resolution imaging fluorometry, and numerical modeling to understand harmful algal bloom transport, submesoscale current impacts, and the development and survival of Antarctic krill larvae. These efforts aim to improve prediction and management of coastal and open‑ocean resources under changing climate conditions.

title: Research Scientist
research_interests: Climate variability, hydrology, Western U.S. drought, sea level rise, regional climate
research_interests_enriched: Daniel Cayan investigates multi‑scale climate variability—from interannual to centennial—in the Eastern North Pacific and the western United States, emphasizing its hydrologic consequences such as drought, atmospheric‑river‑driven floods, sea‑level rise, and wildfire risk. He integrates oceanographic observations, climate‑ensemble modeling, and visual analytics to understand physical mechanisms and to develop instrumentation for ocean monitoring. A distinctive aspect of his work links climate extremes to pediatric health, especially the environmental triggers and clustering patterns of Kawasaki disease. His interdisciplinary approach combines climate science, hydrology, and epidemiology to inform regional risk assessment and adaptation strategies.

title: Research Scientist
research_interests: Physical oceanography, ocean acoustics, internal waves, ocean instrumentation
research_interests_enriched: Robert Pinkel’s research centers on physical oceanography with an emphasis on internal wave dynamics, ocean mixing, and acoustic propagation. He develops and deploys advanced oceanographic instrumentation—including optical‑fiber temperature arrays, hydrographic Doppler sonar, and inexpensive profiling floats—to obtain high‑resolution measurements of temperature, strain, and momentum transport across a range of oceanic environments, from the South China Sea to the Hawaiian Ridge and the Arctic. His work also supports validation of satellite altimetry (SWOT) at sub‑100 km scales.
